paper_title: NTIRE 2026 Challenge on Video Saliency Prediction: Methods and Results

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: This paper presents an overview of the NTIRE 2026 Challenge on Video Saliency Prediction. The goal of the challenge participants was to develop automatic saliency map prediction methods for the provided video sequences. The novel dataset of 2,000 diverse videos with an open license was prepared for this challenge. The fixations and corresponding saliency maps were collected using crowdsourced mouse tracking and contain viewing data from over 5,000 assessors. Evaluation was performed on a subset of 800 test videos using generally accepted quality metrics. The challenge attracted over 20 teams making submissions, and 7 teams passed the final phase with code review. All data used in this challenge is made publicly available - https://github.com/msu-video-group/NTIRE26_Saliency_Prediction.

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: This paper presents an overview of the NTIRE 2026 Challenge on Video Saliency Prediction. The goal of the challenge participants was to develop automatic saliency map prediction methods for the provided video sequences. The novel dataset of 2,000 diverse videos with an open license was prepared for this challenge. The fixations and corresponding saliency maps were collected using crowdsourced mouse tracking and contain viewing data from over 5,000 assessors. Evaluation was performed on subset of 800 test videos using generally accepted quality metrics. The challenge attracted over 20 teams making submissions, and 7 teams passed the final phase with code review. All data used in this challenge is made publicly available https://github.com/msuvideogroup/NTIRE26_Saliency_Prediction. 1. Introduction Visual saliency prediction focuses on approximating the behavior of the human visual system (HVS) by modeling how attention is naturally distributed across scene. Rather than processing all visual information uniformly, humans tend to concentrate on specific regions that stand out perceptually. Saliency prediction models aim to capture and preIn addition to dict these attention patterns automatically. applications in neuroscience and cognitive science, highquality saliency estimation is key component in numer- *A. Moskalenko (and.v.moskalenko@gmail.com), A. Bryncev, I. Kosmynin, K. Shilovskaya, M. Erofeev, D. Vatolin, and R. Timofte were the challenge organizers, while the other authors participated in the challenge. Sec. 6 contains the authors teams and affiliations. NTIRE 2026 webpage: https://cvlai.net/ntire/2026/ ous multimedia applications. It is utilized in tasks such as perceptually guided compression [9, 24, 31, 59, 70], objective quality evaluation [2, 76, 111, 116], adaptive content retargeting [1, 21, 29], media enhancement [64, 83], etc. In addition to traditional images and videos, saliency modeling has been extended to immersive formats, including 360 content, where it facilitates optimized encoding and transmission [97, 101, 118]. Saliency concepts are also applied in the 3D domain, for example, in the mesh simplification task [45, 46, 68], where geometrical details can be selectively preserved in perceptually important regions. 1.1. Saliency Prediction Methods Early approaches to saliency prediction primarily relied on low-level visual cues, including color, contrast, and texture patterns. These statistical properties of natural scenes formed the foundation of many classical methods [32, 36, 40]. With the shift toward video analysis, researchers began to incorporate not only spatial but also temporal information. Video saliency prediction models leveraged dynamic features to capture motion across frames [28, 60, 61]. Modern neural networkbased approaches have significantly improved performance by learning complex spatial [44, 57], temporal [18, 37, 65, 120], and audio [10, 93, 108] patterns directly from data. Newer approaches are also beginning to adapt [11, 91] large-scale Vision Language Models (VLMs) for saliency prediction tasks. In addition to traditional images and videos, saliency modeling has been extended to other domains, including VR/360 content [15, 39, 112, 117] and 3D mesh saliency [17, 62, 114, 115] 1.2. Video Saliency Datasets Saliency prediction aims to estimate continuous saliency map for each frame of an input video. Saliency maps, in turn, have an interpretation of the probability density that given pixel will attract the users attention. Saliency maps are typically derived from fixation maps. Fixation maps, in turn, are obtained by aggregating eye-tracking data from special eye-tracking device on multiple viewers during viewing session. All fixation maps are then smoothed with Gaussian, and the resulting map is normalized to correspond to probability distribution. At present, the largest video datasets containing eye-fixation data are Hollywood2 [63], which includes 1707 videos viewed by 19 participants, and DHF1K [102], which contains 1000 videos annotated by 17 viewers. Collecting eye-tracking data is expensive and difficult to scale, which limits the size of available datasets. To address this, several proxy methods for crowdsourced saliency annotation have been developed. Most of these approaches require only computer mouse to collect saliency stimuli [38, 43, 58, 85, 92], making large-scale crowdsourced data collection feasible. With the aid of various filtering and enhancement techniques, these methods have enabled the creation of large-scale video saliency datasets [66]. In this challenge, we also rely on collecting fixations using mouse movements and have further improved data collection, filtering, and post-processing for obtaining highquality saliency data in crowdsourcing. This challenge is one of the challenges associated with the NTIRE 2026 Workshop 1 on: deepfake detection [34], high-resolution depth [113], multi-exposure image fusion [75], AI flash portrait [27], professional image quality assessment [73], light field super-resolution [106], 3D content super-resolution [100], bitstream-corrupted video restoration [121], X-AIGC quality assessment [53], shadow removal [96], ambient lighting normalization [95], controllable Bokeh rendering [84], rip current detection and segmentation [19], low light image enhancement [13], high FPS video frame interpolation [14], Night-time dehazing [3, 4], learned ISP with unpaired data [71], shortform UGC video restoration [48], raindrop removal for dual-focused images [49], image super-resolution (x4) [12], photography retouching transfer [20], mobile real-world super-resolution [47], remote sensing infrared superresolution [51], AI-Generated image detection [30], crossdomain few-shot object detection [74], financial receipt restoration and reasoning [26], real-world face restoration [98], reflection removal [7], anomaly detection of face enhancement [119], video saliency prediction [67], efficient super-resolution [80], 3d restoration and reconstruction in adverse conditions [52], image denoising [88], blind computational aberration correction [90], event-based image deblurring [89], efficient burst HDR and restoration [69], lowlight enhancement: twilight cowboy [42], and efficient low light image enhancement [110]. 1https://www.cvlai.net/ntire/2026/ 2. Video Saliency Prediction Challenge 2.1. Challenge Dataset We used two publicly available datasets as sources of video content [22, 105]. Both datasets consist of diverse videos curated from YouTube, and all included videos are distributed under an open CC-BY license. As an additional preprocessing step, we retained only video streams with minimum resolution of FullHD. All selected videos were then transcoded using the libx264 codec with constant rate factor (CRF) of 23, converted to 30 FPS, and resized to FullHD resolution. Although all videos shared the same aspect ratio, they could be presented in either horizontal or vertical orientation. Horizontal and vertical videos were sampled at 3:1 ratio. In addition, audio tracks were normalized according to the EBU R128 standard and transcoded to stereo AAC at 256 Kbps. We built on prior work in crowdsourced saliency data collection, including our previous methodology [66], and further refined the procedure through participant filtering as well as additional preand post-processing of fixation data. These improvements brought the quality of the collected data closer to that obtained with an eye-tracker. The obtained challenge dataset consists of 2,000 videos and over 1M frames. Over 5,000 viewers participated in the crowdsourcing data collection, resulting >70 viewers for each video. The average assessor time it takes to complete the 20 main and 3 verification questions is 15 minutes. The average video length in the dataset is 18 seconds. 2.2. Evaluation To objectively evaluate challenge solutions, we employed four widely used metrics [6]: Pearsons Correlation Coefficient (CC), Similarity (SIM), Area Under the Curve (AUCJudd), and Normalized Scanpath Saliency (NSS). For each participant, the final ranking was determined by averaging their ranks across all four metrics on the test set. In cases where two or more methods obtained the same final rank, ties were resolved using the first metric in the listed order for which their scores differed. During the final phase, participants were required to submit their final predictions, factsheet, and the code necessary to reproduce the reported results; these submissions were subsequently verified by the organizers. In total, 7 teams successfully completed this phase. The final evaluation also included the organizers baseline Center Prior method. 2.3. Challenge Phases The challenge dataset was randomly divided into two subsets: training set comprising 1,200 videos with corresponding fixation data and saliency maps provided to participants, and test set containing 800 videos. The test set was further randomly split into public test subset of

During the two intermediate phases, each participants best-performing solution on the public subset was evaluated on 250 videos from the private test subset. In the final phase, all methods were assessed on the entire private test subset. 3. Results This section introduces the results of NTIRE 2026 Video Saliency Prediction Challenge. The values of all metrics and the final ranking are presented in Table 1. In this case, two teams have the same average rank across metrics, and according to the metric priority rules, the team with the highest CC metric value wins. The first-place solution received from the iLearn team (Sec. 4.1). They used shared InternVideo2 [103] backbone with two complementary decoders, and the final saliency map is produced by averaging the two expert predictions. The second-place solution received from the CVSP team (Sec. 4.2). They used large-scale V-JEPA2 [5] backbone, using its self-supervised video representations to estimate saliency from predictive spatiotemporal features. The third-place solution from ARK MMLAB (Sec. 4.3) builds hierarchical model on InternVideo2 [103], extracting multi-level spatiotemporal features and aligning them with feature upsampling module. Vertex team 4.4 extends TMFI [120] by adding bottom-up aggregation path to the original top-down fusion, giving richer multi-scale feature interaction. AAM team 4.5 combines audio-visual fusion, temporal dynamics modeling, and hyperbolic decoder to predict saliency maps. SHU-MIIPLab team 4.6 proposes diffusion-based model that predicts saliency from RGB frames and optical flow using state-space blocks. NTR team 4.7 uses dual-stream design, combining pretrained video backbone for motion cues with pretrained image backbone for fine spatial details. 4. Teams and Methods 4.1. iLearn Figure 1. iLearn Video Saliency Prediction Pipeline. We propose multi-expert ensemble framework [99] for video saliency prediction. As illustrated in Figure 1, our method builds upon shared InternVideo2 [103] backbone and employs two structurally complementary decoders whose predictions are fused to produce the final saliency maps. Shared Backbone. We adopt InternVideo2-Stage2 6B as the spatio-temporal feature extractor. Multi-level features are extracted from four intermediate layers (layers 11, 23, 35, and 47), capturing information ranging from lowlevel spatial details to high-level semantic abstractions. The backbone is fine-tuned with LoRA [35] for saliency prediction, while preserving the pretrained video representations. Expert 1: Temporal-Modulated Decoder with Spatial Priors. The first decoder generates an explicit temporal attention map from the deepest features (layer 47) and uses it to multiplicatively modulate shallower features in topdown manner. At each level, the modulated features are refined through 3D residual blending blocks. Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM) [72] is further applied to inject global conditioning. The output is passed through refinement head augmented with learnable center bias prior, leveraging the well-known tendency of human gaze toward the image center. Expert 2: Multi-Scale Decoder with Deep Auxiliary Supervision. The second decoder projects all four levels of features into unified dimension and fuses them in top-down method via concatenation and 3D residual fusion blocks. temporal gating mechanism is applied at the deepest level to re-weight features across time steps. Crucially, this decoder employs deep auxiliary supervision: independent saliency prediction heads are attached at multiple intermediate levels, providing dense gradient signals that encourage each level to learn meaningful saliency representations. Complementarity and Ensemble. The two experts offer complementary inductive biases. Expert 1 relies on multiplicative gating and explicit spatial priors, excelling at scenes with dominant central subjects and stable temporal dynamics. Expert 2 employs concatenation-based fusion and data-driven multi-scale learning without built-in priors, offering stronger adaptability to complex scenes with off-center or multiple salient regions. To fuse the complementary predictions, we adopt logit-space ensemble. The saliency outputs from both experts are first transformed into logits via the inverse sigmoid function, averaged, and then projected back to the probability space using sigmoid activation. Furthermore, our framework is extensible; while the current implementation focuses on these two experts, additional experts tailored to specific requirements can be integrated into the ensemble in the future. Training Strategy. We adopt two-stage training scheme. In Stage 1, the backbone is frozen and only the decoder is trained to establish strong saliency decoding capability. In Stage 2, LoRA modules are injected into the backbone and jointly fine-tuned with the decoder using lower learning rate. The training objective combines KL divergence, Pearsons correlation coefficient, similarity, and normalized scanpath saliency losses. Each expert is trained independently following this two-stage protocol, and the ensemble is performed at inference time. 4.2. CVSP Human visual attention is strongly guided by an implicit understanding of how the physical world behaves. Our gaze naturally gravitates towards objects that move unexpectedly or violate our expectations about scene. Predictive coding theory [78] interprets this behavior, proposing that the brain minimizes prediction errors relative to generative model of the world. Under this framework, prediction errors drive action to reduce them, and saliency, as driver of attention and eye movements, should therefore correlate with the preFigure 2. An overview of PredJSal. The framework repurposes V-JEPA2 backbone to extract rich spatiotemporal representations, decoded into per-frame saliency maps via 3D convolutional decoder with multi-scale skip connections and lightweight temporal self-attention module. diction errors produced by predictive coding model [87]. This theoretical foundation motivates our use of VJEPA2 [5] as backbone for saliency prediction, since it is trained to predict masked regions of natural videos in learned representation space. V-JEPA2 acquires an implicit understanding of intuitive physics, including object permanence and shape consistency [23], through the same principle of minimizing spatiotemporal prediction error, making its representations substrate for saliency estimation. We therefore present PredJSal [8], predictive JEPAbased framework that repurposes the rich internal representations of V-JEPA2 [5] for video saliency prediction. The overall pipeline is shown in Figure 2. PredJSal is built on V-JEPA2 Giant (ViT-g/16) backbone pretrained with selfsupervised masked video prediction on large-scale video data. Given clip of frames, the encoder produces dense spatiotemporal feature volume F, with intermediate representations extracted at three evenly spaced encoder layers to provide coarse-to-fine features for multi-scale decoding. Before decoding, lightweight self-attention module refines the encoder features along the temporal axis, treating each spatial position as an independent sequence over time to integrate cross-frame context without mixing spatial locations, producing ˆF. 3D convolutional decoder then progressively upsamples ˆF while reducing the temporal dimension to single output frame, with multi-scale skip connections from the intermediate encoder layers fused via element-wise addition at three decoder stages to recover spatial detail lost during encoding. lightweight 2D convolutional head produces our final prediction. To reduce the effect of random initialization, we train our model on four different seeds and average the predicted saliency maps at inference to produce single map. Figure 3. ARK MMLAB video saliency prediction framework. 4.3. ARK MMLAB Our proposed video saliency prediction framework is built upon hierarchical architecture, aiming to effectively capture both spatial semantics and temporal dynamics from video clips. As illustrated in Figure 3, the pipeline consists of visual encoder, feature upsampling module, temporal attention mechanism, and hierarchical decoder. The detailed structures of our method are illustrated as follows. 4.3.1. Visual Encoder the InternVideo2-1B [104] model as our We adopt backbone to extract robust spatiotemporal representations. Given an input video clip of size 3 16 224 224 (Channel Time Height Width), the encoder generates hierarchy of feature maps from four different depth levels, denoted as y1, y2, y3, and y4. These features encapsulate finegrained local details at early stages and abstract high-level semantics at deeper stages. To align the dimensions for subsequent processing, all extracted features are reshaped into 5D tensors of size 1408 16 , where the spatial resolutions are 112 112, 56 56, 28 28, and 14 14. 4.3.2. Feature Upsampling Module To mitigate the spatial resolution discrepancy between different feature levels, we introduce Feature Upsampling Module. This module applies series of bilinear interpolations combined with 2D Convolutions, Group Normalization, and GELU activations. Specifically, y1 is upsampled to spatial size of 128128 with 512 channels, y2 to 6464 with 512 channels, and y3 to 32 32 with 768 channels. The deepest feature y4 is retained at its original resolution (14 14) to preserve the highest level of semantic abstraction. 4.3.3. Temporal Attention and Hierarchical Decoder Before decoding, Temporal Attention mechanism is applied. It takes the deepest feature y4 to compute set of temporal weights using 3D convolutions and temporal pooling. These weights are then multiplied across all feature levels (y1 to y4) to dynamically emphasize the most salient frames within the clip. The Hierarchical Decoder progressively fuses these temporally-weighted features in top-down manner. It consists of four branches corresponding to the four feature levels. Starting from the deepest branch (Branch 4), the features are processed through 3D Convolutions and ReLU activations, upsampled, and then concatenated with Figure 4. Pipeline of the proposed bidirectional enhanced TMFI. We extend TMFI by introducing an additional bottom-up multiscale feature fusion to more comprehensively exploit multi-scale information. In the figure, the orange arrows represent the original top-down fusion in TMFI, while the green arrows denote the added bottom-up fusion. During both fusion processes, the highest-level or lowest-level features are repeatedly utilized, as illustrated by the diagonal arrows in the corresponding colors. the features from the adjacent shallower branch (Branch 3). This concatenation and upsampling process is repeated until Branch 1. Finally, the fused representations are passed through bilinear interpolation layer and series of 2D convolutions to yield the final saliency map of size 224 224. 4.4. Vertex TMFI [120] is one of the state-of-the-art methods for video saliency prediction. It employs Video Swin Transformer [56] to extract four-scale spatiotemporal features, followed by Semantic-Guided Encoder that enhances these features in top-down manner. The enhanced multiscale features are then fed into Hierarchical Decoder to produce corresponding saliency maps, which are fused via 3D convolution to obtain the final prediction. Building upon TMFI, we extend the original top-down enhancement by introducing an additional bottom-up feature aggregation pathway, as illustrated in Figure 4. This bidirectional design enables better utilization of both highlevel semantic information and low-level fine-grained details. Specifically, while TMFI adopts upsampling for topdown fusion, we perform bottom-up aggregation by applying max-pooling to lower-level features for downsampling to align feature resolutions. The aligned features are then combined via element-wise addition and further refined using 3D convolutions for deeper spatiotemporal fusion. As result, the number of feature scales is expanded from 4 to 8, leading to richer representations. Correspondingly, we generate 8 multi-scale saliency maps, which are subsequently fused into the final prediction via learnable weighted aggregation approach, replacing the original 3D convolution for more efficient and stable fusion. Furthermore, we observe strong complementarity across different model paradigms. Therefore, we incorporate additional predictions from DiffSal [109], SalFoM [65], and the Figure 5. (a) Overview of AAM. (b) The general-specific attention ordering between (zanc,ztxt), (ztxt,zimg) is enforced in hyperbolic space using entailment cones. The external angle ϕ of specific condition (ztxt) is pushed to be within the aperture threshold ηω of the general attention (zanc). (c) The FokkerPlanck Dynamics (FPD) Modeling illustrates the drift, diffusion, and correction processes used to model the transition of attention over the video frame axis. original TMFI, and perform unified fusion strategy. The final prediction is obtained through weighted combination of outputs from all these models, followed by explicit postprocessing operations including Gaussian smoothing and temporal smoothing, which improve spatial coherence and reduce temporal inconsistencies across consecutive frames. 4.5. AAM We present the Attend to Anything Model (AAM), which represents human attention as shared latent process across modalities, scenes, and time by modeling hierarchical semantic specialization and temporal dynamics, as shown in Figure 5. Visual inputs are encoded by frozen selfsupervised backbone (DINOv3 [86]) with LoRA for adaptation to attention modeling. Text prompts and audio signals are encoded using frozen CLIP [77] and Wav2CLIP [107] encoders, respectively, with audio mapped into the visual semantic space. Audio-visual fusion is performed through relevance-gated cross-attention mechanism, ensuring that audio cues contribute only when semantically aligned. For video inputs, frame-wise attention representations are refined by FokkerPlanck Dynamics (FPD) module that models attention evolution over spatiotemporal manifold. Visual and textual representations are lifted into hyperbolic space via hierarchical entailment learning for explicit hierarchy enforcement. Finally, geometry-aware hyperbolic decoder projects structured representations back to Euclidean space to generate spatial attention maps. 4.6. SHU-MIIPLab We propose SSF-DiffNet, novel State Space Fusiondriven Diffusion Network for video saliency prediction. SSF-DiffNet is denoising diffusion model [33, 81]. The inputs are an RGB frame and its corresponding optical flow map generated by Flow-Anything [50]. Figure 6. Proposed SSF-DiffNet pipeline, which consists of state space condition generation network and denoising network. As illustrated in Figure 6, SSF-DiffNet consists of two major components: denoising network tailored for generating saliency maps and state space condition generation network that works with the denoising network. The state space condition generation network is constructed based on the state space model [25, 54]. Specifically, we first adopt the SAM2 encoder [79] (i.e. Hiera [82]) equipped with adapters as the feature extraction backbone for RGB and optical flow. Then, we utilize RGB to guide optical flow through Semantic Injection Mamba Block (SI-MB), thereby transmitting semantic attributes to motion cues to identify salient regions. Subsequently, we integrate RGB with optical flow through Spatiotemporal Alignment Mamba Block (SA-MB). The SA-MB aligns the appearance and motion features and narrows their discrepancies, generating high-quality conditions to guide the denoising network. In the denoising network, we reuse SIMB to transmit texture cues and semantic priors from these highly informative conditions to the noisy mask, producing accurate saliency maps through iterative optimization. With all components collaborating tightly, our SSF-DiffNet achieves competitive performance. 4.7. NTR 4.7.1. Dual-Stream Network Architecture We propose Pretrained Dual-Stream Saliency Network that jointly exploits spatiotemporal motion cues from video stream and fine-grained spatial appearance cues from frame stream. Both streams are initialized from domainspecific pretrained weights, enabling the network to leverage large-scale video and image priors without requiring additional pretraining on saliency data. 4.7.2. Video Stream The video stream processes clip of L=8 frames as input. We use R(2+1)D-18 [94], factored spatiotemporal convolutional network pretrained on the Kinetics-400 [41] action recognition dataset. The (2+1)D factorization separates 3D convolutions into spatial 2D and temporal 1D components, improving both training efficiency and motion modeling. Given an L-frame clip RL3H , the R(2+1)D backbone produces feature maps at four spatial scales: {V (1), (2), (3), (4)} = R(2+1)D-18(C), where (s) RTsCsHsWs has temporal dimension Ts that decreases with depth. To obtain spatial feature maps, we apply temporal softmax pooling: the temporal logits are computed as learned linear projection of the channel dimension, softmax-normalized across the Ts frames, and used to compute weighted sum: (s) = (cid:80)Ts , α(s) = softmax(w(s) (s)), yielding temporally aggregated features (s) RCsHsWs. 4.7.3. Frame Stream The frame stream processes only the center frame It to capture high-resolution spatial appearance. We use ConvNeXtTiny [55], pretrained on ImageNet-1K [16], which pro- {P (1), (2), (3), (4)} = vides 4 feature stages: ConvNeXt-Tiny(It), where (s) RC sHsWs . The ConvNeXt features provide complementary static-scene context that complements the motion-focused R(2+1)D features. j=1 α(s) (s) 4.7.4. Decoder with Center Prior Fusion The decoder merges the two streams across all four scales via learned 11 projections followed by concatenation and 33 convolutions. Starting from the coarsest scale and progressing to the finest, each decoder stage fuses: D(s) = Conv(cid:0)[ (s), (s), Up(D(s+1))](cid:1), where [] denotes channel-wise concatenation and Up() is bilinear upsampling. At the decoder output, we fuse Gaussian center prior [0, 1]H [40]. The center prior is precomputed 2D Gaussian that captures the well-known center bias of human gaze. The final saliency logit is: ˆSt = σ(cid:0)D(1) + β G(cid:1), where σ is the sigmoid function and β is learned scalar weight. The output is bilinearly upsampled to the original video resolution. 4.7.5. Loss Function We combine four complementary losses: = λKL LKL + λCC LCC+λNSS LNSS+λBCE LBCE, with λKL=1.0, λCC=0.7, λNSS=0.3, λBCE=0.1. KL divergence LKL measures the information loss between predicted and ground-truth saliency distributions. Correlation coefficient LCC = 1 CC( ˆSt, St) directly penalizes low correlation. NSS LNSS = NSS( ˆSt, Ft) rewards high normalized scanpath saliency at fixation locations. Fixation BCE LBCE applies binary cross-entropy with positive class weight 8.0 to the binary fixation map Ft, sharpening saliency at fixation sites. 4.7.6. Warm-Start Training Schedule Training proceeds in two stages, each warm-starting from the previous best checkpoint. Both stages use AdamW with cosine annealing. The backbone learning rate is set to 0.1 the head learning rate to avoid disrupting the pretrained spatiotemporal and image features. Table 2. Training configuration. Parameter Stage 1 Stage 2 Init from Epochs Batch size Input size Clip length Frame stride Clips/video LR (head) LR (backbone) Weight decay Grad clip Train/Val split ImageNet/K400 4 8 2242 8 frames 2 4 2104 2105 104 1.0 1100/100 Stage 1 best 3 8 2242 8 frames 2 4 1104 1105 104 1.0 1100/100 4.7.7. Inference Each output frame is predicted using an 8-frame clip (L=8, stride 2) centered on that frame, processed at 224224 resolution. The batch size is 8 clips per forward pass. Test-time augmentation (TTA): We apply horizontal flip TTA each clip is processed in its original orientation and horizontally flipped, then the two predicted saliency maps are averaged after flipping the second back to the canonical orientation. This halves variance from left-right saliency asymmetries. Output: Per-frame saliency maps are bilinearly upsampled to the original video resolution, min-max normalized per video, and written as grayscale MP4 (all 3 BGR channels equal) at the original FPS. 4.8. Baseline Solution This solution was provided by the organizers team as baseline. To obtain the Center Prior prediction, we averaged all saliency maps from all 1,200 training videos, and then fitted Gaussian function centered at the center of the frame, optimizing only the σx, σy parameters. The fitting target was L2 loss between the two images. The resulting Center Prior was duplicated for all frames for all test videos and given to the participants as sample submission. 5. Conclusion This paper presented an overview of the NTIRE 2026 Challenge on Video Saliency Prediction. We introduced novel dataset of 2,000 videos, accompanied by saliency annotations collected from more than 5,000 assessors through crowdsourced mouse tracking. In the final phase, 7 teams proposed their solutions and passed the code-sharing phase. The final results show that top solutions are built on large pretrained video backbones and multi-scale spatiotemporal modeling. We believe that the described methods and challenge dataset will support future research toward more accurate video saliency prediction models. 6. sections.title: Acknowledgments
sections.content: The work of Andrey Moskalenko, Alexey Bryncev, and Dmitry Vatolin was financially supported by the Institute for Artificial Intelligence of Lomonosov Moscow State University. The evaluation was carried out using the MSU270 supercomputer of the Lomonosov Moscow State University. Crowdsourcing assessors were hired using Yandex.Tasks. The submission system was partially supported by the Humboldt Foundation, OPPO, Kuaishou, and the University of Wurzburg (Computer Vision Lab).
